html:
PRIMOCO UAV SE (PRIUA.PR)

At CZK 810 on May 18, 2026, Primoco is a CZK 3.8 billion market-cap Prague-listed UAV manufacturer. The first correction is important: this is not the massive late-2025 dilution story in the stale draft. The company still shows 4,708,910 shares, unchanged share capital, and no equity-raise proceeds in 2025. Cash is also not a liquidation floor. Q1 cash was CZK 315 million, or only about CZK 67 per share, with no bank debt but meaningful customer advances. The stock has to work on earnings power, not a fake net-cash arbitrage.

The real thesis is forward earnings. Screens still see a lumpy micro-cap with 2024-2025 revenue down from the 2023 spike. That is the wrong denominator. Primoco’s 2025 orders exceeded CZK 1.06 billion for 42 aircraft, but only 16 aircraft were delivered in 2025, leaving a large revenue tail for 2026. Q1 2026 then came in as the best first quarter in company history, with CZK 100.5 million revenue, CZK 59.3 million EBITDA, and CZK 46.4 million net profit, plus a reported backlog of 30 aircraft worth CZK 850 million. That backlog alone supports the Wood/FactSet-style 2026 setup: roughly CZK 920 million revenue, CZK 369 million EBITDA, and CZK 274 million net income, moving to about CZK 1.24 billion revenue and CZK 360 million net income in 2027.

At today’s price, that is about 14x 2026 earnings and 10.5x 2027 earnings. On EV/EBITDA, using roughly CZK 3.5 billion EV, it is about 9.5x 2026 and 7x 2027. That is not dirt cheap on stale trailing numbers, but it is cheap if Primoco is shifting from a Ukraine-driven delivery spike to a certified European defense procurement platform.

The moat is certification and operating permission, not just airframe hardware. The One 150 has NATO STANAG 4703 military certification and EASA LUC civil authorization, and the company claims a rare ability to operate in regulated airspace rather than only in improvised battlefield conditions. It has delivered more than 250 aircraft across four continents, owns its Písek airport, and is building out training, service, and production infrastructure. The new Písek facility, with planned investment around CZK 600 million, is expected to lift capacity to up to 300 aircraft per year by 2028. The market is discounting that as speculative capex. The upside case is that procurement demand catches up before the plant opens.

The variant perception is that Primoco is not a commodity drone maker. Small tactical drones are crowded, but certified medium fixed-wing ISR platforms with 15-hour endurance, 30 kg payload, NATO airworthiness, training capability, and civil airspace approval are a narrower category. Spain’s Guardia Civil order, worth about EUR 4 million, was delivered in less than a month and validates non-Ukraine European demand. The Airbus cooperation around autonomy and target recognition, and early Rheinmetall weaponized-platform work cited in Wood’s report, are not in the base case, but they create real option value.

Retail context is also favorable. This is not a crowded meme defense trade. The public discussion is thin, often just European retail investors asking where the stock can even be traded and debating founder control/free float. That obscurity matters because execution can rerate the stock before broad institutions notice.

The main bear risk is not balance-sheet failure. It is that demand proves episodic. Ukraine exposure, single-product concentration, founder control, thin Prague liquidity, and aggressive capacity expansion all deserve a discount. Customer advances also mean cash is partly tied to future delivery obligations, not freely distributable. The company must keep winning new NATO and security-force contracts fast enough to fill a much larger factory.

I would underwrite it as a high-upside, execution-dependent defense compounder rather than a “cheap net cash” stock.

Bear case: CZK 520, down 36%. Deliveries slip, backlog is not replenished, 2026 revenue lands closer to CZK 550-600 million, and the market values the business at a small-cap lumpy contractor multiple.

Base case: CZK 1,450, up 79%. 2026 revenue approaches CZK 900-950 million, 2027 revenue reaches about CZK 1.2 billion, EBITDA margins hold near 38-40%, and the stock trades at roughly 18x 2027 EPS for a certified, debt-free European UAV platform.

Bull case: CZK 2,400, up 196%. NATO/security-force follow-on orders, Airbus-enabled autonomy, or a Rheinmetall-style weapons integration push deliveries toward 70-90 units by 2028, with EPS power above CZK 130 and a strategic-defense multiple.
